---
description: "Use when building or modifying PV Lakehouse code. Enforces Clean Code, architecture boundaries, English-only code, no icons, standard FastAPI plus Jinja plus PostgreSQL plus Trino stack, main/dev/stg/prod environment structure, and environment plus gitignore policies."
name: "PV Lakehouse Delivery Standards"
applyTo: "**/*.py, **/*.jinja, **/*.j2, **/*.html, **/*.sql, **/*.yml, **/*.yaml, **/Dockerfile, **/*.sh, **/*.ps1, **/.env*, **/.gitignore, **/*.md"
---
# PV Lakehouse Delivery Standards

## 1) Mandatory Environment Structure
Use this environment layout at repository root:

```text
main/   # canonical source structure
dev/    # development overrides and local configs
stg/    # test/uat overrides, mirrors production behavior
prod/   # production-only deployment and runtime configs
```

Use this baseline skeleton under `main/`:

```text
main/
	backend/app/{api,schemas,services,repositories,core}
	frontend/{templates,static/{css,js,img}}
	sql/{bronze,silver,gold,serving}
	docker/{postgres,trino,scripts}
```

Rules:
- Keep business logic and shared source code in `main/`.
- Keep `dev/`, `stg/`, and `prod/` focused on environment-specific config, secrets references, deployment manifests, and runtime overrides.
- Keep environment config folders aligned: `dev/config`, `stg/config`, and `prod/config`.
- Do not duplicate application source code across `dev/`, `stg/`, and `prod/`.
- Promote changes in order: `main` -> `dev` -> `stg` -> `prod`.

## 2) Required Technology Stack
- Backend framework: FastAPI.
- Frontend rendering: Jinja templates.
- Primary OLTP database: PostgreSQL running with Docker.
- Query engine for analytical access: Trino running with Docker.

Rules:
- Do not introduce alternative frameworks unless explicitly approved.
- Keep Docker as the default local and CI runtime for PostgreSQL and Trino.
- Use PostgreSQL for transactional metadata and operational tables.
- Use Trino for analytical and ad-hoc SQL workloads over curated datasets.

## 3) RBAC and Module Ownership Context
Respect role boundaries when generating endpoints, templates, and workflows.

- Data Engineer: pipeline operations, data quality, ingestion and transformation controls.
- ML Engineer: training, model comparison, model promotion workflows.
- Data Analyst: analytics and SQL query workflows over curated data.
- Viewer: dashboard and forecast consumption.
- Admin: cross-module configuration and governance.

## 4) Architecture Rules
Follow a layered architecture and keep responsibilities separated.

Backend conventions (inside `main/backend`):
- `api/`: route handlers and request/response wiring.
- `schemas/`: Pydantic input/output models.
- `services/`: domain business logic.
- `repositories/`: database and query access.
- `core/`: settings, security, and shared infrastructure.

Dependency rules:
- Flow must be `api -> services -> repositories -> data source`.
- `api/` must not execute SQL directly.
- `services/` must not import from `api/`.
- `repositories/` must not contain business orchestration logic.

Frontend conventions (inside `main/frontend`):
- `templates/`: Jinja templates grouped by module.
- `static/`: CSS, JS, and assets.
- Use Jinja inheritance to avoid duplication.

Data conventions:
- Preserve Medallion naming and flow: Bronze -> Silver -> Gold -> Serving.
- Keep SQL and data jobs aligned with those layers.

Query conventions:
- Route analytical queries through Trino-oriented repository/query services.
- Keep operational writes and transaction-oriented workloads in PostgreSQL repositories.

## 5) Clean Code Requirements
- Write small, single-responsibility functions.
- Keep modules cohesive and avoid cross-layer shortcuts.
- Use clear naming and explicit typing in Python.
- Validate inputs at API boundaries and fail fast with clear errors.
- Add tests for meaningful behavior changes.
- Avoid dead code and commented-out blocks.

## 6) Language and Writing Rules
- Write code, identifiers, comments, logs, and commit messages in English.
- Keep user-facing UI text in English.
- Do not use icons or emoji in code comments, logs, commit messages, UI labels, or documentation generated by the assistant.

## 7) Module Context to Respect
When generating UI routes, templates, APIs, or query models, align with these project modules:
- Dashboard
- Data Pipeline
- Data Quality
- ML Training
- Model Registry
- Forecast
- Analytics
- Solar AI Chat
- Settings (planned)

## 8) Environment Promotion Gates
Before promoting changes between environments:

- `main` must contain canonical source changes.
- `dev` must validate integration behavior and local Docker stack usage.
- `stg` must validate test/uat behavior and release candidates.
- `prod` must only include production-safe config and approved runtime settings.

## 9) Delivery Safety Checklist
Before finalizing changes:
- Confirm stack compliance (FastAPI, Jinja, PostgreSQL Docker, Trino Docker).
- Confirm architecture boundaries are respected.
- Confirm no icons and all code-related text is English.
- Confirm changes are placed in the right environment layer (`main/dev/stg/prod`).
- Confirm no unnecessary duplication across environments.

## 10) Environment Files Policy
Use explicit environment file conventions to avoid leaking secrets and to keep setup reproducible.

- Keep real secrets only in `.env` files that are ignored by Git.
- Provide a committed template file named `.env_example` for each runtime location that needs env variables.
- Keep a committed requirement document named `.env.requirements.md` that explains each variable, expected format, and whether it is required.
- Ensure variable names are consistent across Docker Compose, application settings, and scripts.
- Never hardcode secrets, API tokens, SMTP passwords, or private keys in source files.

## 11) Git Ignore Policy
Follow these ignore rules for repository safety and cleanliness.

- Ignore all runtime `.env` files by default.
- Keep `.env_example` and `.env.requirements.md` tracked in Git.
- Ignore temporary extraction folders, local artifacts, and generated logs.
- Do not ignore SQL, migration, or source code files required for reproducibility.
